5.2: veatta Beit-Lechem Efrata tzair lihyot be'alfei Yehudah mimcha li yetze lihyot moshel beYisrael umotza'otav mikedem miyemei olam: 'But you, O Bethlehem Ephrathah, who are too little to be among the clans of Judah, from you shall come forth for me one who is to be ruler in Israel, whose coming forth is from of old, from ancient days.' Motza'otav mikedem miyemei olam (whose coming forth is from of old, from ancient days/from ancient times/from everlasting): the preposition and nouns can point to either the antiquity of the Davidic dynasty (David's line goes back to ancient Bethlehem) or to the eternal pre-existence of the coming ruler. The NT reading (John 8:58; John 1:1) takes it in the latter, stronger sense: the one born in Bethlehem has his 'goings forth' from eternity.
5.1: Now muster your troops, O daughter of troops — atah titgodedi bat-gedud: wordplay — gedud (troops/raiding party) echoes mitgodedi (muster yourself). The siege is underway: matsor sam 'aleinu (a siege has been laid against us). Ba-shevet yakkbu 'al-ha-lechi et shofet Yisra'el (with a rod they strike the ruler of Israel on the cheek) — striking the cheek was the ultimate public humiliation (1 Kgs 22:24; Job 16:10; Lam 3:30); the ruler's humiliation by the enemy sets up the contrast with the coming ruler from Bethlehem (v2).
5.3: Therefore he will give them up until the time when she who is in labor gives birth — lachen yitnem 'ad-'et yoledah yaladah: the labor-birth motif from 4:9-10 continues; the birth-pangs of exile precede the delivery of restoration. Ve-yeter echav yashuvu (the rest of his brothers shall return) — the restoration of the scattered northern tribes alongside the southern remnant; the re-unification of all Israel under the Bethlehem ruler.
5.4: He shall stand and shepherd his flock in the strength of the LORD — ve-'amad u-ra'ah be-'oz YHWH: three elements of the coming ruler's activity: 'amad (stand, be established), ra'ah (shepherd/feed), and the strength-source — be-'oz YHWH (in YHWH's strength) and bi-ge'ut shem YHWH Elohav (in the majesty of the name of the LORD his God). Ve-yashavu ki-atah yigdal ad-afssei arets (and they shall dwell secure, for now he shall be great to the ends of the earth) — global greatness of the shepherd-ruler.
5.5: And this one shall be their peace — ve-hayah zeh shalom: a remarkable statement — the person himself (zeh = this one) is shalom (peace, wholeness). Peace is not an attribute of his rule but his identity. The Assyrian invasion that follows in vv5b-6 is countered not by superior military force but by the one who embodies shalom. Shiva'h ro'im u-shmonah nesikhei adam (seven shepherds and eight human commanders) — the graduated numerical formula (x, x+1) from Amos 1-2 indicating sufficiency; seven-plus-eight represents complete adequacy of response.
5.6: They shall rule the land of Assyria with the sword, and the land of Nimrod — Nimrod (the land of Nimrod) is Mesopotamia/Babylon (Gen 10:8-12); by v5's time Babylon had absorbed the Assyrian empire. The prophecy operates on two levels: immediate (the Assyrian crisis of 701 BC) and eschatological (the ultimate defeat of all anti-covenant powers represented by Assyria/Nimrod).
5.7: The remnant of Jacob shall be in the midst of many peoples, like dew from the LORD — ke-tal me'et YHWH: dew (tal) in the ANE was a gift of divine provision — it came without human effort, unpredictably, sustaining vegetation when rain was absent. Ka-revivim 'alei esev (like showers on the grass) — the remnant's presence among nations is beneficial and life-giving, not aggressive. Asher lo-yekavveh le-ish ve-lo yayachel li-vene adam (which do not wait for a man or tarry for the children of man) — the blessing comes from divine initiative alone, not human arrangement.
5.8: The remnant of Jacob shall be among the nations, like a lion — the two remnant-images (vv7-8) are strikingly contrasted: the same remnant is both dew (gentle, life-giving) and lion (powerful, destroying). Ka-arye be-vehemot ya'ar (like a lion among the beasts of the forest) and ke-khefir be-'adrei tson (like a young lion among flocks of sheep) — the lion tears and there is no rescuer. The duality of the remnant's role reflects the dual nature of the Messiah's coming: gentle shepherd and consuming judge.
5.9: Your hand shall be lifted up over your adversaries — terum yadkha 'al-tsarrekha: the uplifted hand of victory; the standard gesture of military triumph (Exod 17:11). Ve-khol-oyvekha yikkarettu (and all your enemies shall be cut off) — comprehensive enemy-elimination.
5.10: And in that day, declares the LORD, I will cut off your horses from among you — ve-hikhrati susekha: the divine purification of Israel begins with military hardware. Susim (horses) and merkavot (chariots) were prohibited to the Israelite king by Deut 17:16 ('he shall not acquire many horses for himself'); relying on cavalry was a form of covenant unfaithfulness. The cutting off of military power is not punishment here but liberation from false security.
5.11: I will cut off the cities of your land and tear down all your strongholds — ve-hikhrati 'arei artsekha: the fortified cities and strongholds (mibtsar) represent the defensive infrastructure built instead of trusting YHWH. Each thing cut off in vv10-14 corresponds to something that replaced genuine covenant trust.
5.12: I will cut off sorcery from your hand — ve-hikhrati khesafim: keshafim (sorcery, magical practices) — divination and magic as alternative means of knowledge/control that competed with prophetic revelation. Ve-'ovenat lo-yihyu lakh (you shall have no more fortune-tellers) — 'onen (fortune-teller, cloud-reader, omen-interpreter) — the complete elimination of the divinatory infrastructure.
5.13: I will cut off your carved images and your stone pillars — pesilim (carved images) and matstsevot (sacred standing stones/pillars) — the matstsevah (standing stone) was originally acceptable (Gen 28:18; 31:45) but became associated with Canaanite fertility worship and prohibited (Deut 16:22). Ve-lo-tishtachaveh od le-ma'aseh yadekha (you shall no longer bow down to the work of your own hands) — the criterion: handmade objects that receive worship.
5.14: I will root up your Asherah poles from among you and destroy your cities — asherayikh (Asherah poles) — wooden poles or stylized trees representing the Canaanite goddess Asherah, consort of El; planted near altars (Deut 16:21; 1 Kgs 14:23; 16:33). The consistent coupling of Baal and Asherah worship throughout Kings confirms their paired popularity. Their rooting up (nateshthi) is thorough agricultural clearance.
5.15: And in anger and wrath I will execute vengeance upon the nations that did not obey — ve-'asiti be-af u-ve-chamat naqam et-ha-goyim: the divine purification that began with Israel's own military/cultic structures (vv10-14) turns outward to the nations. The double emotion — af (anger/nose) and chamath (heat, wrath) — intensifies the final judgment. The nations are held accountable to the same moral standard as Israel.

7.1: ʾallĕlî lî kî hāyîtî kĕʾaspĕsuppê-qayiṣ kĕʿōlĕlōt bāṣîr: “Woe is me! I am like a gleaner at the summer harvest, like one picking through the remnants of the vintage.” The opening ʾallĕlî is the lament-cry of the prophetic speaker; the harvest metaphors convey total exhaustion of moral resources — the summer harvest is stripped bare, and the gleaner finds only remnants (ʿōlĕlōt, the dropped clusters left for the poor by law, Lev 19:10; Deut 24:21). bikkûrāh ʾiwwĕtāh napšî — “my soul craved the first-ripe fruit (bikkûrāh)” — the first-ripe figs of early summer are the OT’s symbol of the best and most desired produce (Hos 9:10: “like the first-ripe fig at the beginning of the season”); the prophet’s soul hungers for a faithful person and finds none. The harvest-exhaustion metaphor frames the social diagnosis of vv. 2-6.
7.2: ʾābad ḥāsîd min-hāʾāreṣ wĕyāšār bāʾādām ʾayin: “The faithful (ḥāsîd) have vanished from the land; there is no one upright (yāšār) among people.” Ḥāsîd is the covenant-loyal person, characterized by ḥesed (steadfast love) — the same root as YHWH’s own defining attribute (v. 18: ḥāpeṣ ḥesed hûʾ). Its disappearance from the land is the covenant catastrophe: the community whose hallmark is ḥesed has lost its ḥăsîdîm. kullām lĕdāmîm yeʾĕrōbû (“they all lie in ambush for blood”) — dāmîm is the plural of blood, indicating bloodshed or blood-guilt (cf. 2 Sam 3:28); yeʾĕrōbû from ʾārab = to lie in wait/ambush. The hunting-net image (yāṣûdû ʾîš ʾet-ʾāḥîw bĕḥērem, “each hunts his brother with a net”) inverts the covenant of brotherhood.
7.3: ʿal-hārāʿ kappayim lĕhêṭîb haśśar šōʾēl wĕhaššōpēṭ baššillûm: “They are diligent at doing evil — the prince demands a bribe, the judge accepts a payment.” ʿal-hārāʿ kappayim lĕhêṭîb is a compressed phrase: “for evil, [using] their hands, to do well/excel at it” — they have perfected the art of evil. The three corruptors (prince, judge, powerful man) operate as a syndicate: the powerful man (haggādôl) articulates the demand (habbîa ʿnapšô hîʾ, “states the craving of his soul”) and together they “weave/twist” it (wayyĕʿabbĕṭûhā — from ʿābat, to twist or distort; the verb is rare and the text difficult; the image is of a scheme woven from multiple threads of corruption).
7.4: ṭôbām kamĕmēr yāšār min-mĕsûkāh: “The best among them is like a briar; the most upright is sharper than a thorn hedge.” The superlative corruption: even the best (ṭôbām) in this society is a briar (mĕmēr — a thorny briar-plant, elsewhere ḥēdaeq). The thorn-hedge (mĕsûkāh) is the standard barrier against intruders — thorny plants used for fencing. The social fabric, which should be like clothing that protects, has become only thorns that injure. yôm mĕṣappêkā pĕquddātĕkā bāʾāh — “the day your watchmen warned of — your time of judgment — has arrived”; the “watchmen” (mĕṣappêkā) are the prophets who warned of exactly this (cf. Isa 52:8; Ezek 3:17) — pĕquddāh = appointed time/visitation/reckoning. ʿattāh tihyeh mĕbûkātām (“now they are thrown into bewilderment”) — the rulers who were confident face sudden confusion.
7.5: ʾal-taʾamînû bĕrēaʿ ʾal-tiṭĕḥû bĕʾallûp: “Put no trust in a neighbor; place no confidence in a close friend.” Rēaʿ = neighbor/companion (the covenant relationship of Lev 19:18: “love your neighbor”); ʾallûp = a close companion, intimate friend (used in Ps 55:13 of a trusted companion who betrays). The breaking of social trust at every level — neighbor, companion, even spouse (“guard what you say even from the one who lies in your arms”, šōkevet ḥêqekā) — mirrors the complete social breakdown of vv. 2-4. This extreme counsel (not trusting anyone) is prophetic description of a social condition, not a timeless maxim against trust.
7.6: kî-ben mĕnabbēl ʾāb bat qāmāh vĕʾimmāh kallāh bĕḥōtantāh: “For the son treats his father with contempt, the daughter rises against her mother, the daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law.” The household-division catalogue reaches its climax: ʾōyĕbê ʾîš ʾanšê bêtô — “a person’s enemies are the members of their own household.” Jesus cites this verse directly in Matt 10:35-36: “I have come to bring not peace but a sword. For I have come to bring division — a son against his father, a daughter against her mother, a daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law. A man’s enemies will be those of his own household.” Jesus takes Micah’s description of social collapse and applies it to the division that his proclamation will produce — not as the goal of his mission but as its inevitable effect in a world resistant to the kingdom. Luke 12:51-53 parallels with “Do you think I came to give peace on earth? No, I tell you, but rather division.”
7.7: wĕʾānî bYHWH ʾăṣappeh ʾôḥîlāh lēʾlōhê yišʿî: “As for me, I will watch expectantly for the LORD; I will wait for the God who saves me.” After the social chaos of vv. 1-6, the individual speaker pivots on the strong adversative wĕʾānî (“but as for me” — the same pronoun-pivot as Ps 73:23; Ps 5:7). ʾăṣappeh from ṣāpāh = to watch/look out (the watchman's posture); ʾôḥîlāh from yāḥal = to wait with hopeful expectation. ʾĕlōhê yišʿî (“the God of my salvation”) — yišʿî = my salvation (root yšʿ, also in Jonah 2:9 and the name Jesus/Yeshua). The pivot from communal despair (vv. 1-6) to personal trust (v. 7) is the structure of prophetic lament — the “but as for me” that refuses to be defined by the surrounding conditions.
7.8: ʾal-tišmĕḥî ʾōyabtî lî kî nāpaltî qamtî kî-ʾēšēb baḥōšek YHWH ʾôr lî: “Do not gloat over me, my enemy. Though I have fallen, I will rise. Though I sit in darkness, the LORD is a light for me.” The individual voice (v. 7) now addresses an enemy — whether Assyria, Babylon, or any hostile nation is deliberately left general. Two profound antitheses: (1) kî nāpaltî qamtî — “though I have fallen, I will rise” — the qāmtî (I will arise/rise) is the verb of resurrection and awakening (Dan 12:2: yāqîṣû; Isa 26:19: “your dead shall live; their bodies shall rise”); (2) ʾēšēb baḥōšek YHWH ʾôr lî — darkness-to-light by YHWH’s presence. Isaiah 9:2 (cited in Matt 4:16) uses the same darkness-to-light structure for the Messianic arrival. The ʾôr lî (“light to me”) is YHWH himself as light — cf. Ps 27:1: “YHWH is my light and my salvation.”
7.9: zaʿap YHWH ʾeśśāʾ kî ḥāṭāʾtî lô ʿad ʾăšer yārîb rîbî wĕʿāśāh mišpāṭî: “I will endure the LORD’s anger, for I have sinned against him, until he takes up my case and brings justice for me.” The legal language is precise: yārîb rîbî (“he will argue/contend my case”) — YHWH as the gōʾēl (kinsman-redeemer) and legal advocate who takes up the case of the one wrongly accused. rîb is the covenant-lawsuit term used throughout the prophets (Mic 6:2; Jer 2:9; Hos 4:1). wĕʿāśāh mišpāṭî (“brings justice for me”) — the mišpāṭ YHWH establishes is both vindication of the wronged party and condemnation of the oppressor. yōṣîʾēnî lāʾôr (“he will lead me into the light”) resumes the darkness-light of v. 8: the light is YHWH’s just verdict made visible.
7.10: wĕtirʾeh ʾōyabtî ûtĕkassehā bûšāh: “My enemy will see it; shame will cover the one who taunted me.” The reversal: the one who mocked becomes the one shamed. The taunt (haggîd ʾelay ʾayyē YHWH ʾĕlōhāyik, “who said to me, ‘Where is the LORD your God?’”) is the standard theodicy-taunt of enemies (Ps 42:3, 10: “Where is your God?”; Ps 79:10: “Why should the nations say, ‘Where is their God?’”; Joel 2:17). The taunt assumes YHWH’s absence from the humiliated people; the reversal (“my own eyes will look upon her; now she will be trampled like mud”, tîrāmās kĕṭît ḥûṣôt) is the vindication that silences the taunt. The mud-trampling image echoes Mic 7:17 (nations lick dust like snakes).
7.11: yôm libnôt gĕdērāyik yôm hahûʾ yirḥaq ḥōq: “A day is coming for rebuilding your walls; on that day the foreign decree will be lifted far away.” The restoration oracle: libnôt gĕdērāyik — “to build your walls” (the walls of the city, whether Jerusalem’s literal walls or the walls as metaphor for protection and sovereignty). yirḥaq ḥōq (“the decree will be lifted far”) — the ḥōq here is the foreign decree of empire (perhaps the Assyrian or Babylonian administrative/tribute law imposed on vassal states); its removal signals genuine restoration of sovereignty. The “day” (yôm hahûʾ) is the eschatological “that day” of prophetic hope.
7.12: yôm hahûʾ wĕyābōʾ ʾādêkā lĕminnî ʾaššûr wĕʿārê miṣrayim: “In that day people will come to you from Assyria and the cities of Egypt — from Egypt to the Euphrates, from sea to sea and from mountain to mountain.” The universal scope of restoration: the streaming of peoples from Assyria and Egypt (the two great empires that dominated Israel’s history) to Zion parallels Isaiah 2:2-3 and Mic 4:1-2 (the Micah passage almost identical to Isa 2). The miyyām ʿad-yām ûmihhar ʿad-hāhār (“from sea to sea and mountain to mountain”) is the idiom for universal scope — the same as Ps 72:8 (“may he have dominion from sea to sea”), applied to the messianic king. The international pilgrimage to Zion that Mic 4 announced is here restated in the context of personal and national restoration.
7.13: wĕhāyĕtāh hāʾāreṣ lĕšammāh ʿal-yōšĕbeyhā mippĕrî maʿallêhem: “But the land will become desolate because of its inhabitants — the result of their deeds.” A parenthetical note of judgment within the restoration oracle: the land (hāʾāreṣ) — the land from which the peoples stream in v. 12, or perhaps the whole earth — will experience desolation (šammāh) proportional to its inhabitants’ deeds (mippĕrî maʿallêhem, “from the fruit of their deeds” — the harvest-consequence image resuming v. 1). The agricultural metaphor (fruit → desolation) closes the harvest imagery of vv. 1-4.
7.14: rĕʿēh ʿammĕkā bĕšibṭekā ṣōʾn naḥălātĕkā: “Shepherd your people with your staff — the flock of your inheritance — who dwell isolated in a forest surrounded by fertile land.” The prophet addresses YHWH directly: rĕʿēh (“shepherd!” — the imperative of pastoral care). Šibeṭ = staff/rod/scepter — simultaneously the shepherd’s crook and the ruler’s scepter (Ps 2:9; 23:4); the two meanings coexist. ṣōʾn naḥălātĕkā (“flock of your inheritance”) — YHWH’s people as his naḥălāh (inheritance/possession), echoing Deut 9:26 (“do not destroy your people, your inheritance”). šōknî lĕbādād yaar bĕtôk karmel — “who dwell isolated in a forest surrounded by fertile land” — the isolation of the remnant in difficult terrain, waiting for YHWH’s restoration. Bashan and Gilead = the ideal northern pastures (cf. Jer 50:19; the Transjordanian highlands famous for lush grazing).
7.15: kimê ṣēʾtkā mēʾereṣ miṣrayim ʾarʾennû niplāʾôt: “As in the days when you came out of the land of Egypt, I will show them wonders (niplāʾôt).” YHWH responds (the speaker shifts from prophet to YHWH, or is understood as YHWH in the prophet’s vision): the new redemption will be structured like the Exodus. Niplāʾôt (wonders/miracles — from the root pālaʾ, “to be extraordinary/wonderful”) is the technical term for the Exodus plagues and sea-crossing (Exod 3:20; Ps 78:12; 106:7). The new Exodus promise — structured identically to the first — is a central NT category: Jesus’s transfiguration (“departure”/exodos, Luke 9:31), his feeding miracles (Exodus-manna typology), and his death and resurrection as the ultimate Exodus fulfillment.
7.16: yirʾû gôyim wĕyēbōšû mikkōl gĕbûrātām: “The nations will see and be put to shame despite all their power.” The vision of universal shame (bōšâ) in the face of YHWH’s powerful restoration: the nations who taunt (“where is your God?”, v. 10) see the wonders (v. 15) and are silenced. yāśîmû yād ʿal-peh (“they put their hand over their mouth”) — the gesture of speechlessness before the overwhelming; used in Job 29:9 (the great honor the elders showed Job); 40:4 (Job’s response to YHWH from the whirlwind). ʾāzněhem tĕḥĕraš (“their ears will be stopped/deafened”) — the silence extends to their hearing as well as their speaking.
7.17: yĕlaḥăkû ʿāpār kannāḥāš kĕziḥălê ʾereṣ yirgĕzû mimmisĕgĕrôtêhem: “They will lick the dust like a snake, like creatures that crawl on the earth; they will come trembling out of their fortresses.” The snake-and-dust image deliberately reverses Gen 3:14 (the curse on the serpent: “you will eat dust all the days of your life”) — the nations take the serpent’s posture of defeat. Yĕlaḥăkû ʿāpār (“lick the dust”) is the posture of total submission; Ps 72:9 uses it of the king’s enemies: “may his enemies lick the dust.” yipḥădû ʾel-YHWH ʾĕlōhênû wĕyirʾû mimmekā (“they will turn in dread to the LORD our God and will be in fear of you”) — the nations’ submission is not yet salvation; it is the fear of overwhelming power that precedes (or stands apart from) covenant inclusion.
7.18: mî-ʾēl kāmôkā nōśēʾ ʿāwôn wĕʿōbēr ʿal-pešaʿ lišʾērît naḥălātô: “Who is a God like you, forgiving iniquity and passing over transgression for the remnant of his inheritance?” The name Mîkāh means “who is like YHWH?” (mî kāh/mî kamōhû) — the book’s own name is embedded in this climactic question. Mî-ʾēl kāmôkā is the rhetorical answer to YHWH’s uniqueness: no one. The divine action is described with two verb phrases: (1) nōśēʾ ʿāwôn (“bearing/lifting iniquity”) — the same root (nāśāʾ) as Isa 53:12: “he bore the sins of many” (wĕhûʾ ḥēṭʾ-rabbîm nāśāʾ); the bearing-iniquity language of the Suffering Servant pre-echoed here; (2) ʿōbēr ʿal-pešaʿ (“passing over transgression”) — ʿābar in this context = overlooking, not requiring payment for (cf. Prov 19:11). kî-ḥāpeṣ ḥesed hûʾ (“for he delights in steadfast love”) — YHWH’s ḥesed is his ḥēpeṣ (desire/delight), not a reluctant concession.
7.19: yāšûb yĕraḥămēnû yikbōš ʿăwōnōtênû wĕtašlîk bimĕṣûlôt yām kol-ḥaṭṭōʾtām: “He will again have compassion on us; he will trample our iniquities underfoot. You will hurl all our sins into the depths of the sea.” Three verbs of divine action over sin: (1) yāšûb yĕraḥămēnû — “he will return to have compassion” (šûb + raḥam); (2) yikbōš ʿăwōnōtênû — “he will subdue/conquer our iniquities” (kābaš = to tread down, subdue; used of the creation mandate in Gen 1:28 and of military conquest); the iniquities are treated as an enemy to be conquered; (3) wĕtašlîk bimĕṣûlôt yām kol-ḥaṭṭōʾtām — “you will hurl (tašlîk) all their sins into the depths of the sea.” The verb šālak (hurl/throw) is the same root as Jonah 1:15 (throwing Jonah into the sea) and Jer 36:23 (throwing Jeremiah’s scroll into the fire). The depths of the sea (mĕṣûlôt yām) = the place of no return; the Exodus sea that swallowed Pharaoh’s army (Exod 15:5) becomes the place where YHWH hurls sin itself. This verse became the basis for the Jewish practice of Tashlikh (casting bread on water on Rosh Hashanah) and is the OT’s most vivid image of the totality of forgiveness.
7.20: tittēn ʾĕmet lĕYaʿăqōb ḥesed lĕʾAbrāhām ʾăšer-nišbaʿtā laʾăbōtênû mîmê qedem: “You will be faithful (ʾĕmet) to Jacob and show steadfast love (ḥesed) to Abraham, just as you swore to our fathers in days long past.” The final verse anchors the entire eschatological hope in the patriarchal covenants. ʾĕmet (faithfulness/truth) for Jacob and ḥesed (steadfast covenant love) for Abraham — the two covenantal attributes of YHWH (Exod 34:6: “abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness”, rav ḥesed wĕʾĕmet) applied to the two patriarchs. Nišbaʿtā (you swore by oath) — the Abrahamic covenant confirmed by divine oath (Gen 22:16-17: “by myself I have sworn”; Heb 6:13-18: the oath guarantees the promise unchangeable). Paul builds his entire theology of the gospel upon exactly this: the promise to Abraham (ḥesed lĕʾAbrāhām) is fulfilled in Christ, who is Abraham’s seed (Gal 3:16), and through whom all nations receive the blessing sworn to the fathers (Gal 3:14). The book of Micah ends where Luke 1:72-73 begins: “to show the mercy (ἔλεος = LXX’s rendering of ḥesed) promised to our fathers and to remember his holy covenant, the oath that he swore to our father Abraham.”

1.1: Micah the Morasthite — Micah was from Moresheth (or Moresheth-gath, 1:14), a village in the Shephelah (Judean foothills) about 25 miles southwest of Jerusalem — a rural agricultural community, in contrast to his contemporary Isaiah who was urban and courtly. His ministry spans three Judean kings: Jotham (750-735 BC), Ahaz (735-715 BC), and Hezekiah (715-686 BC) — the period of Assyrian imperial expansion under Tiglath-pileser III, Shalmaneser V, and Sennacherib. The address is to both Samaria and Jerusalem: both capitals are indicted.
1.2: Hear, all you peoples — shim'u ammim kulam: the universal summons opening a covenant lawsuit (rib). Ve-tiqshev erets (listen, O earth) — the earth personified as a witness; cf. Deut 32:1 ('Listen, O heavens; give ear, O earth'). The divine witness formula — Adonai YHWH bakh le'ed (the Lord GOD will be a witness against you) — casts YHWH in the double role of prosecuting party and cosmic witness. Me-heikhal qodsho (from his holy temple) — the heavenly throne-room is the legal venue.
1.3: The LORD is coming out of his place — ki hinneh YHWH yotse mimmekomo: the divine departure (yatsa') from his dwelling is theophanic language; YHWH does not leave heaven lightly. Yarad ve-darakh al-bamotei arets (he will come down and tread upon the high places of the earth) — bamot (high places) are both cultic hilltop shrines and the literal highlands of Canaan; the divine tread on the high places is at once a demolition of idolatrous shrines and a theophanic stride across the landscape.
1.4: The mountains will melt under him — ve-nimesu heharim tachtav: the standard language of theophanic poetry (Ps 97:5; Nah 1:5). The two similes: ke-donag mipnei ha-esh (like wax before the fire) and ke-mayim muggarim bemored (like waters rushing down a steep slope) — wax melts under direct heat; water rushes irreversibly downhill. The irreversibility of each image suggests the divine theophany cannot be stopped once initiated.
1.5: Because of the transgression of Jacob — be-pesha' Ya'aqov kol-zot: pasha' (transgression, rebellion against authority) is the leading accusation term in Micah. The theological equation that follows is sharp: Jacob's transgression = Samaria (not just located in Samaria, but is Samaria — the capital as the embodiment of national sin); Judah's high places = Jerusalem. The capitals are not incidental locations but the concentrated expression of the nation's covenant failure.
1.6: I will make Samaria a ruin heap in the open country — ve-samtiv le-'iy hasade: 'iy hasade (ruin heap in an open field) — the word 'iy can mean heap of rubble or pile of stones; the phrase denotes a city demolished to open farmland. Le-mataa' karem (a planting ground for vineyards) — agricultural de-urbanization; where a capital once stood will be vineyard terraces. Ve-higgarti la-gai avaneha (I will pour her stones down into the valley) — the stones of Samaria's buildings tumbled down the steep hill on which the city stood.
1.7: All her carved images shall be shattered — ve-khol-pesileyha yukkatu: the three elements of Samaria's cultic apparatus: pesilim (carved idols), ethnah (prostitution wages/fees — the sacred prostitution system), and 'atsabbim (idols). The key is ethnah — the term for the fee paid to a temple prostitute, also used in Deut 23:18 ('you shall not bring a prostitute's wage into the house of the LORD'). The accusation: Israel's idolatry was financed by sacred prostitution; the wealth gathered through fertility-cult prostitution returns to the same system — the cycle is self-perpetuating and self-destroying.
1.8: I will wail and lament; I will go stripped and naked — Micah describes his own mourning performance. Sholal ve-'arom (stripped and naked) — removing outer garments as a sign of extreme mourning or solidarity with those going into exile; Isaiah performed a similar prophetic action (Isa 20:2-3). Ke-tannim (like jackals) and ke-venot ya'anah (like ostriches) — both animals were associated with wilderness lamentation sounds; jackals howled at night; the ostrich's cry was associated with desolation (Job 30:29; Isa 34:13).
1.9: Her wound is incurable — ki anushab makoteiha: anush (incurable, desperate, grievous) is the same root as the name Enosh; used for wounds beyond remedy (Jer 15:18; 17:16). The wound has spread: ki va'ah ad-Yehudah (it has come to Judah) and naga' ad-sha'ar ammi (reached even to the gate of my people) — Samaria's disease is not contained at the border; it has infected Judah and is at Jerusalem's gate. The wound-and-infection metaphor anticipates Sennacherib's 701 BC campaign.
1.10: Declare it not in Gath; weep not at all — be-Gat al-taggidu: the opening of the city-lament series (vv10-15) echoes David's lament over Saul: 'Tell it not in Gath' (2 Sam 1:20). Micah adapts the Davidic lament for the Assyrian catastrophe. What follows is a sophisticated chain of wordplays (paronomasia) on Shephelah town names: each town's name resonates with its designated fate, a rhetorical technique that makes the judgment memorable by linking geography to destiny. Be-Bet le-'Aphrah hitpallashi afar (in Beth-le-aphrah roll in the dust) — Aphrah echoes afar (dust); a town named for dust will indeed be in the dust.
1.11: Pass on in nakedness and shame, inhabitants of Shaphir — the wordplays continue. Shaphir (beauty) — the beautiful town goes naked and shameful; beauty inverted. Lo yatsa'ah yoshevet Tsa'anan (the inhabitants of Zaanan do not come out) — Tsa'anan echoes yatsa' (go out); those in the 'going-out' town cannot go out. Mispad Bet ha-'Etsel yiqqach mikkem 'emdat (Beth-ezel mourns — it can no longer support you) — Etsel means 'beside, nearby'; the 'nearby' town is no longer near/helpful.
1.12: The inhabitants of Maroth wait anxiously for good news — ki chalah le-tov yoshevet Marot: Marot echoes mar (bitter) or mara (bitterness); the bitter-named town waits for tov (good) and receives disaster instead. Ki-yarad ra' me'et YHWH le-sha'ar Yerushalam (disaster has come down from the LORD to the gate of Jerusalem) — the disaster (ra') comes not from Assyria but from YHWH himself; the Assyrian is merely the instrument.
1.13: Harness swift steeds to the chariot, O inhabitants of Lachish — Lakhish echoes larekesh (swift steeds/chariot horses). Lachish was the second-largest city in Judah and a major military fortress; its mention here is historically precise — Sennacherib's Lachish reliefs (now in the British Museum) depict the Assyrian siege of 701 BC in detail. Reshit chat'at hi le-vat Tsiyon (you were the beginning of sin for the daughter of Zion) — Lachish is accused of being the entry point for Israel's syncretism (possibly because of its strategic position on the trade routes from Philistia).
1.14: Give parting gifts to Moresheth-gath — shillukhim Moresheth-Gat: shillukhim (parting gifts, the gift given when a bride leaves her father's house; cf. 1 Kgs 9:16) — Moresheth-gath, Micah's own hometown, is being 'given away' to Assyria like a bride leaving home. Batei Akhziv le-akhzav (the houses of Achzib shall be a deception) — Achzib echoes akhzav (deception, dried-up stream — a wadi that promises water but runs dry in summer); the deceptive town will not fulfill the hope placed in it.
1.15: I will again bring a conqueror against you, inhabitants of Mareshah — Mareshah echoes yoresh (possessor, conqueror); the town whose name means 'possessor' will have a new possessor brought against it. Ad-Adullam yavo' kevod Yisra'el (the glory of Israel shall come to Adullam) — Adullam, the cave where David hid from Saul (1 Sam 22:1-2), becomes the refuge again; Israel's glory has retreated from the exposed cities to the wilderness cave.
1.16: Shave your head and make yourself bald for your precious children — qarchah ve-gazzi 'al-benei ta'anugayikh: head-shaving was a mourning rite for the dead (Deut 14:1; Job 1:20; Amos 8:10); done for 'precious children' going into exile, the living are mourned as if dead. Harchivi qorchateikh ka-nesher (make your baldness as wide as the eagle's) — the eagle (or possibly vulture, nesher) was known for its bald head; the baldness of mourning should match the eagle's natural baldness. Ki galu mimmeikh (for they have gone from you into exile) — the exile is spoken of as already accomplished.

2.1: Woe to those who plan iniquity — hoy choshevei aven ve-po'alei ra': the hoy (woe) oracle targets premeditated sin — plotting evil on their beds at night and executing it at dawn. Ba-or ha-boqer ya'asuha ki yesh-le'el yaddam (at the dawn of morning they carry it out, because it is within their power) — yesh-le'el yaddam (it is in the power of their hands) — they have the social and economic power to execute what they planned; power enables injustice.
2.2: They covet fields and seize them — ve-chamdu sadot ve-gazalu: the accusation echoes the Decalogue's final prohibition (lo tachmod — you shall not covet, Exod 20:17; Deut 5:21) and its consequence in the Naboth narrative (1 Kgs 21). Ve-'ish u-veito nasau (and a man and his house) — the legal unit of a man's household (bayit). Nachalah (inheritance) is the covenant land grant: inalienable under Mosaic law (Lev 25:23-28; Num 36:7-9); seizing it violated both the Decalogue and the land-tenure laws. The latifundialization of the land (rich consolidating small farms) is the specific economic crime targeted.
2.3: I am devising disaster against this family — hineni choshev al-hamishpachah hazot ra'ah: the same verb (chashav = plan, devise) used against the wicked planners in v1 is now used of YHWH's counter-planning. The lex talionis is structural: they planned evil (choshevei aven); YHWH devises evil against them (choshev ra'ah). Lo-telkhu romah (you shall not walk haughtily) — the upright, proud posture (romah) of the powerful will become impossible under the yoke of judgment.
2.4: In that day a taunt shall be raised against you — ba-yom hahu yissa' 'aleykhem mashal: the taunt-song (mashal) inverts the property-seizure: chelq ammi yamir (the portion of my people is exchanged) — chelq (portion, allotment) is the covenant land share given by lot; yamir (is exchanged) — the fields they seized are redistributed to others. La-shoveiv sadoteinu yechalleq (to the faithless he apportions our fields) — the redistributor (shoveiv = turncoat, apostate, rebel) now holds the fields the covenant people once owned; the judgment mirrors the crime.
2.5: You shall have no one to cast the lot in the assembly of the LORD — lachen lo-yihyeh lekha mashliakh chevel be-goral: the goral (lot) was the mechanism for distributing covenant land in the original division under Joshua (Num 26:55-56; Josh 14-21). To have no one casting the lot in the qahal YHWH (assembly of the LORD) means exclusion from the covenant community's land-rights — they who seized others' lots lose their own participation in the covenant distribution system.
2.6: Stop preaching! — so they preach — al-tittfu (do not drip / do not prophesy) — nataf (to drip, to let words drip down) is used for prophetic speech (Amos 7:16; Ezek 21:2); the word-picture of prophecy as falling rain/dew. The opponents command silence: al-tittfu to the prophets. The irony: they are preaching (yattifun) the command not to preach — they themselves become preachers while silencing others.
2.7: Is the Spirit of the LORD cut short? — ha-qatsar ruach YHWH: the divine Spirit cannot be curtailed (qatsar = shortened, constrained). The rhetorical questions defend genuine prophecy: YHWH's Spirit is not cut short; his words do good to those who walk uprightly. Ha-elo de-varav yeitivu (do not my words do good to him who walks uprightly?) — the criterion: YHWH's genuine words bring good to the upright, not just to those who want to hear comfortable oracles.
2.8: My people have risen up as an enemy — ve-etmul ammi le-oyev yaqum: the people's behavior toward their own members constitutes treating them as enemies. Shalmat ta'deru (you strip the cloak from) — ta'der (strip off, peel away) applied to the salmah (outer garment/cloak) — stripping the cloak of those returning from war who want only peace (shuvi milchamah betach). The outer cloak was the poor person's only bedding; cf. Exod 22:26-27; Amos 2:8.
2.9: The women of my people you drive out from their pleasant houses — neshei ammi tegarshun mibet ta'anugeha: widows and women of forcibly seized estates are expelled from their homes. Me'al 'olaleha tiqchu hadari le'olam (from their young children you take away my blessing forever) — hadar (beauty, adornment, blessing) taken from young children permanently; the dispossession of the next generation from the covenant blessing.
2.10: Arise and depart, for this is not your place of rest — qumu lekhu ki lo-zo hamenuchah: menuchah (resting place, rest) is the covenant-land term (Deut 12:9; Ps 95:11; 132:14). The land as 'rest' for YHWH's people is being cancelled: be'ur tumme'ah (because of its uncleanness) — ritual-cultic defilement of the land through injustice and idolatry (cf. Lev 18:24-28; Num 35:33-34) removes its status as covenant menuchah.
2.11: If someone walks in a spirit of wind and deceit — lulei ish holekh ruach ve-shaqer kizzev: the false prophet is described by his medium — spirit (ruach) = wind, and deception (shaqer). Ettof lekha la-yayin ve-lash-shekhar (I will preach to you of wine and strong drink) — nataf (drip/preach) again; the false prophet promises material abundance (wine, beer). This is what the people want to hear — comfortable prosperity oracles — making such a prophet popular. The sarcasm is bitter.
2.12: I will surely gather all of you, O Jacob — asof e'esof Ya'aqov kulakh: the double infinitive absolute construction (asof e'esof) expresses absolute certainty of the gathering. Kaqqor be-dovrah ke-'eder be-tokh hadderotam (like sheep in a fold, like a flock in its pasture) — two shepherd images; dor (pasture enclosure) and derotam (their folds). The pastoral reversal of the exile's scattering. The noisy multitude (tehimena me-adam) signals restored community after judgment.
2.13: The one who breaks through goes up before them — alah ha-poretz lifneihem: poretz (the one who breaks through, breach-maker) is a striking messianic-shepherd image. Military language: breaking through enemy lines. Yafretsu va'avru sha'ar (they break out, pass through the gate) — YHWH's people burst through the city gate of exile. Malcham lifneihem ya'avor (their king goes before them) and ve-YHWH be-ro'sham (the LORD at their head) — the king and YHWH in tandem at the front of the procession; the new exodus imagery with YHWH as the divine warrior going ahead of his people.

3.1: Hear, O heads of Jacob and rulers of the house of Israel — shim'u-na ro'shei Ya'aqov u-qetsine beit Yisra'el: the second major summons in the book, now directed specifically at the civil leadership. Ha-lo lakhem lada'at et-ha-mishpat (is it not for you to know justice?) — mishpat (justice, proper judgment) is precisely their assigned role; they are indicted for failing at their own job description. The rhetorical question assumes a 'yes' answer: of course they know mishpat — they are the ones charged with administering it.
3.2: You who hate what is good and love what is evil — sone'ei tov ve-'ohavei ra': the emotional inversion; the moral vocabulary of love/hate applied to ethical categories (cf. Amos 5:15). Gorze'ei 'oram me'aleihem (who tear the skin from my people) — gazar (to cut, tear) applied to skin-tearing; the metaphor of cannibalism begins with this gesture. The leaders' economic exploitation is rendered as literal devouring of the people's bodies.
3.3: Who eat the flesh of my people and strip their skin — the cannibalism metaphor intensifies across three parallel lines: 'asher akhal she'er ammi (who eat the flesh of my people), ve-'orem me'aleihem hipshitu (strip their skin from them), ve-et-atsmotam pitstseihu (break their bones in pieces), u-farshu ka'asher bassir (chop them up like meat in a pot). The cooking-pot image (siyar / qallachat) is precise: the poor are butchered and cooked by the rich the way animals are processed for food. This is the most graphic economic-injustice metaphor in the OT.
3.4: Then they will cry out to the LORD, but he will not answer them — az yiz'aqu el-YHWH ve-lo ya'aneh otam: the covenant reversal: in distress the wicked cry to YHWH, but YHWH's face is hidden. Yastir panav meihem (he will hide his face from them) — divine face-hiding (hester panim) is the removal of covenant presence and protection (Deut 31:17-18; Ps 30:7; Isa 54:8). Ka'asher hera'u ma'allehem (because their deeds have been evil) — the hiding is proportional to their treatment of the poor.
3.5: The prophets who lead my people astray — ha-nevi'im ha-mathi'im et-ammi: false prophets operate on an economic basis: nashakh be-shineihem (who cry 'Peace' — lit. 'bite with their teeth') when they have something to eat — the biting/eating suggests they receive payment in kind. Ve-qiddeshupilchamah (but declare war against the one who gives them nothing to eat) — for those who do not feed them, the oracle changes to judgment. The prophetic word is for sale; the content determined by the client's payment.
3.6: Therefore it shall be night for you, with no vision — lachen layil lakhem me-chazyon: divine judgment on false prophets takes the form of prophetic darkness — no vision (chazyon), no divination (qesem). Va'avah hashemesh al-ha-nevi'im (the sun shall set over the prophets) — losing the prophetic light/sun is the withdrawal of the capacity for divine communication. Va'achshakh aleihem ha-yom (and the day shall go dark over them) — the sun that illuminated genuine vision goes out; the false prophets lose even their false light.
3.7: The seers shall be ashamed, and the diviners put to shame — ve-voshu ha-chozim ve-chapher ha-qosmim: chozeh (seer, visionary) and qosem (diviner — using lots, entrails, or other means) — both categories of professional oracle-givers are silenced. Ve-'atfu kol-levat sefam (they shall all cover their mouths) — the mouth-covering gesture was used for uncleanness (Lev 13:45; Ezek 24:17) and shame; the professional prophets cover their mouths because there is nothing left to say: ein ma'aneh Elohim (there is no answer from God).
3.8: But as for me, I am filled with power — ve-ulam anokhi male'ti khoach: the emphatic contrast (ve-ulam) distinguishes Micah from the false prophets. His threefold equipment: ruach YHWH (Spirit of the LORD), mishpat (justice), and gevurah (strength/might). This is Micah's prophetic self-authorization against the opposition in vv6-7. Le-haggid le-Ya'aqov pish'o (to declare to Jacob his transgression) — the content of his prophetic task is specifically the announcement of pesha' (transgression), not comfortable oracles.
3.9: Hear this, you heads of the house of Jacob — the third summons in the book; the same leadership addressed in 3:1. Ha-mete'avim mishpat (who despise justice) — ta'av (abhor, despise utterly) for the very thing they are supposed to administer. Ve-et kol-ha-yesharah ye'aqqeshu (and make crooked all that is straight) — yaqqash/aqash (make crooked, twist) applied to what is yesharah (straight, right). The leaders geometrically distort the moral-legal landscape.
3.10: Who build Zion with blood and Jerusalem with wrongdoing — boneh Tsiyon be-damim u-Yerushalam be-'avlah: the capital's physical construction is financed by damim (blood, bloodshed — plural) and 'avlah (wrongdoing, injustice). The magnificent temple-city is built on a foundation of violence and injustice — a structural indictment: the building materials are the proceeds of oppression.
3.11: Her rulers judge for a bribe; her priests teach for payment; her prophets give oracles for money — the three institutions that should maintain covenant life are all corrupt: ro'sheha be-shochad yishpotu (rulers judge for bribes), kohaneiha be-mechir yoruhu (priests teach for payment), nevi'eha be-keseph yiqsemu (prophets divine for money). The three-fold institutional corruption covers the totality of covenant leadership. Their self-assurance follows: halo YHWH be-qirbeinu (is not the LORD among us?) — false confidence in divine presence despite corrupt practices. Lo-tavo' aleinu ra'ah (disaster will not come upon us) — the fatal presumption that covenant status provides immunity from judgment.
3.12: Therefore because of you Zion shall be plowed as a field — lachen biglalekhem Tsiyon sadeh techaresh: the most famous verse in Micah and one of the most remarkable in the OT, cited verbatim in Jer 26:18 as the text that saved Jeremiah's life: the elders argued that Micah had prophesied this destruction under Hezekiah, and Hezekiah did not put him to death — establishing the precedent that prophets of doom should not be killed. Ve-Yerushalam 'iyyim tihyeh (Jerusalem shall become a heap of ruins) and ve-har ha-bayit le-vamot ya'ar (and the mountain of the temple a wooded height) — complete deforestation and depopulation of the temple mount.

4.1: But in the latter days it shall come to pass that the mountain of the house of the LORD shall be established — ve-hayah be-acharit ha-yamim (in the latter/last days): the eschatological temporal frame, contrasting with the immediate judgment of 3:12. Micah 4:1-3 is nearly identical to Isa 2:2-4; whether Micah borrowed from Isaiah or vice versa, or both drew from a common prophetic tradition, is debated. Yihyeh nakhin har-beit YHWH be-rosh he-harim — the mountain of the LORD's house raised as the highest mountain is cosmological symbolism: Zion elevated above all competing centers of gravity, political and religious.
4.2: Many nations shall come and say, Come, let us go up to the mountain of the LORD — the voluntary pilgrimage of nations to Zion contrasts with the coerced submission of ANE imperial treaties; nations come willingly to learn YHWH's ways. Yamorenu mi-derakhav ve-nelkhah be-orchotav (that he may teach us his ways and we may walk in his paths) — the teaching is practical life-instruction, not abstract theology. Ki mi-Tsiyon tetse Torah (for from Zion shall go forth instruction/Torah) and u-devar-YHWH mi-Yerushalam — Zion as the universal source of teaching, reversing the Babel-scattering pattern.
4.3: They shall beat their swords into plowshares and their spears into pruning hooks — ve-kittetu charvotem le-ittim ve-chanitoteihem le-mazmeirot: the metalworking reversal image: the forge that produced weapons (charev = sword, chanit = spear) now produces agricultural tools (et = plowshare, mazmeirah = pruning hook). Lo-yissa' goy el-goy cherev ve-lo-yelimdu od milchamah (nation shall not lift up sword against nation, neither shall they learn war anymore) — the cessation of military education and practice; war's institutional structure dismantled.
4.4: Each one under his vine and under his fig tree — ve-yashvu ish tachat gapno ve-tachat te'enato: the vine-and-fig-tree image is the standard OT shorthand for covenant shalom — self-sufficiency, security, agricultural fruitfulness, absence of fear (1 Kgs 4:25; Zech 3:10; cf. also the Elijah/juniper contrast in 1 Kgs 19:4). Ve-eyn machared (and no one shall make them afraid) — the absence of the terror-by-night characteristic of war-zones. Ki pi YHWH Tseva'ot dibber (for the mouth of the LORD of hosts has spoken) — the divine utterance-formula guarantees the vision.
4.5: For all the peoples walk each in the name of its god — ki kol-ha-'ammim yelkhu ish be-shem elohav: an honest acknowledgment of present religious pluralism. Va-anachnu nelekh be-shem YHWH Eloheinu le'olam va'ed (but we will walk in the name of the LORD our God forever and ever) — the counter-declaration of covenant fidelity. The verse creates a deliberate contrast between the present (each nation walks its own path) and the eschatological vision (vv1-3, nations streaming to Zion); in the present, Israel's task is faithfulness to YHWH regardless of what others do.
4.6: In that day, declares the LORD, I will assemble the lame — ha-tsole'ah (the lame) — those who are physically disabled and marginalized. Three groups: tsole'ah (lame), hadde-nichah (driven away/expelled ones), and asher hare'oti (those I have afflicted). The last group is significant: YHWH acknowledges that he himself afflicted some of those he now gathers — divine agency in both the judgment (affliction) and the restoration (gathering).
4.7: And the lame I will make a remnant — ve-samti et-ha-tsole'ah le-she'erit: the she'erit (remnant) — the core survival concept in the prophets — consists precisely of those who were considered weakest. Ve-ha-nehalah le-goy atzum (and the cast-off ones, a strong nation) — the reversal: the expelled become the strong nation. U-malakh YHWH 'aleihem be-har Tsiyon (and the LORD will reign over them in Mount Zion) — the divine kingship over the gathered remnant; the restored Zion as YHWH's throne.
4.8: O tower of the flock, hill of the daughter of Zion — Migdal-'Eder (Tower of the Flock) is a specific place name: Gen 35:21 locates it near Bethlehem, just beyond where Rachel died in childbirth. The shepherd-tower for watching over flocks near Bethlehem becomes a metaphor for the watchpost of Jerusalem's shepherded community. Adah ha-mamlakhah le-vat Yerushalam (the kingdom of the daughter of Jerusalem) — adah (come to, reach) — the former dominion (memshelet ha-rishonah) returns to this center.
4.9: Now why do you cry aloud? Is there no king in you? — lama atta tari'a: the rhetorical question punctures the lamentation: why the panic? The implied answer: because your king and counselor are gone — military-political leadership has failed. Ki-achazakh chil ke-yoledah (for pain has seized you like a woman in labor) — labor-pain is the signature metaphor for the shock of unexpected disaster (cf. Jer 4:31; 6:24; 30:6). Here it introduces the exile-and-return sequence of vv10-13.
4.10: Writhe and groan, O daughter of Zion, like a woman in labor — the labor imagery continues: not merely pain but productive pain that leads to birth. Ki-atah tetse'i min-ha'ir ve-shakhan't ba-sadeh (for now you shall go out from the city and dwell in the open country) — the sequence: city → open country → Babylon. Ve-ba't ad-Bavel (you shall go to Babylon) — a remarkable specific prophecy from an 8th century prophet; Micah preaches approximately 100 years before Babylon becomes the dominant power. Sham tinnatseli sham yig'alekh YHWH (there you shall be rescued; there the LORD will redeem you) — rescue in the place of captivity, not from a distance.
4.11: Now many nations are assembled against you — ve-atah ne'esfu 'alayikh goyim rabbim: the threat from the nations. Their taunt: tetechannaf ve-techaz be-Tsiyon 'eneinu (let her be defiled, and let our eyes gloat over Zion) — techannaf (be defiled, profaned) — the nations want to see Zion desecrated; their gloating gaze is the opposite of the holy pilgrimage of 4:2.
4.12: But they do not know the thoughts of the LORD — ve-hemma lo yad'u machashevot YHWH: divine intention is hidden from the attacking nations. Ki qibbets otam ka-'amir gornah (for he has gathered them like sheaves to the threshing floor) — the image is judicial-agricultural: YHWH gathers the attacking nations the way a farmer gathers grain, not to save them but to thresh them. The attacking nations think they are victors; they are harvest material.
4.13: Arise and thresh, O daughter of Zion — qumi ve-dushi bat Tsiyon: after being told to writhe (v10) and seeing the nations gathered (v12), Zion is commanded to thresh. Ki-qarnekh asim barzel ve-parposotayikh asim nechushshah (for I will make your horn iron and your hoofs bronze) — the horn (qeren) and hoofs (parsa) of a threshing animal (ox) made of iron and bronze — divine reinforcement of the human instrument. Ve-hacharamti la-YHWH bilam (and you shall devote their gain to the LORD) — cherem (devoted/consecrated to YHWH) — the spoil is wholly consecrated, not used for self-enrichment.

6.1: Hear now what the LORD says: Arise, plead your case before the mountains — shim'u-na et-asher YHWH omer: the third major lawsuit (rib) opening in the book. Qum riv et-he-harim (arise, plead your case before the mountains) — YHWH summons Israel to a cosmic courtroom where the mountains serve as ancient witnesses (cf. Deut 32:1; Isa 1:2). The mountains are addressed as yesode arets (enduring foundations of the earth) — witnesses from creation's beginning.
6.2: Hear, O mountains, the LORD's indictment — shim'u harim et-riv YHWH: the mountains are summoned to hear and validate YHWH's legal case. Ve-ha-etanim mosdei arets (you enduring foundations of the earth) — etanim (perennial, enduring — same root as the ever-flowing stream in Amos 5:24); the most permanent features of creation are the witnesses. Ki-riv le-YHWH im-ammo (for the LORD has an indictment against his people) — the rib (legal controversy, lawsuit) pattern established in OT prophetic rhetoric.
6.3: O my people, what have I done to you? In what have I wearied you? Testify against me! — ammi meh-'asiti lakh u-mah hel'etikha aneih-vi: YHWH turns the lawsuit into a direct appeal — almost a reverse lawsuit: testify against me if you have a complaint. Hel'etikha (I have wearied you) — la'ah (to tire, weary, be burdened); YHWH challenges Israel to name any way in which covenant faithfulness to him was an unreasonable burden. This is perhaps the most intimate and vulnerable divine speech in the OT prophets.
6.4: For I brought you up from the land of Egypt and redeemed you from the house of slavery — ki he'elitikha me-erets Mitsrayim: the exodus recital as YHWH's defense — evidence of faithfulness. Ve-et Mosheh Aharon u-Miryam shalachthi lefanekha (and I sent before you Moses, Aaron, and Miriam) — the only place in the OT where Miriam is listed alongside Moses and Aaron as a leader of the exodus (cf. Exod 15:20-21). YHWH's defense includes the full leadership team he provided.
6.5: Remember what Balak king of Moab devised, and what Balaam the son of Beor answered him — the Balaam episode (Num 22-24): Balak hired Balaam to curse Israel; YHWH turned every curse into blessing. Mi-ha-Shittim 'ad-ha-Gilgal (from Shittim to Gilgal) — the two pilgrimage sites bracketing the Jordan crossing: Shittim (east bank, last camp before crossing) and Gilgal (west bank, first camp after crossing, Josh 4:19-5:12). The crossing itself is YHWH's saving act. Lema'an da'at tsidqot YHWH (so that you may know the saving acts of the LORD) — tsidqot YHWH (righteous acts/saving acts) — the plural tsidqot covers all YHWH's historical acts of covenant faithfulness.
6.6: With what shall I come before the LORD, and bow myself before God on high? — bamah aqaddem YHWH: the worshiper's question, escalating through five increasingly extravagant offerings. Ha-eqqaddem be-'olot be-'agalim bene-shanah (shall I come with burnt offerings, with year-old calves?) — the most standard sacrifice begins the escalation. The rhetorical movement is designed to show that the problem is not the inadequacy of the offering-type or quantity.
6.7: Will the LORD be pleased with thousands of rams, with ten thousand streams of oil? — ha-yirtsah YHWH be-alfei elim: the scale escalates to absurdity — ten thousand (riveve) rivers of oil. Then the climax: ha-etten bekori be-pish'i peri vitni le-chatat nafshi (shall I give my firstborn for my transgression, the fruit of my body for the sin of my life?) — the firstborn child (bekori) as the ultimate sacrifice. The reference may target the Moabite king Mesha's child sacrifice (2 Kgs 3:27) or the Canaanite practice of molk offerings, which Israel was explicitly prohibited from doing (Lev 18:21; 20:2-5).
6.8: He has shown you, O man, what is good — higgid lekha adam mah-tov: the answer to the five-step escalation of vv6-7. YHWH has already made clear what he requires — the emphatic higgid (he has declared, made known) points back to Torah and prophetic instruction already given. The three requirements: asot mishpat (to do justice — mishpat = procedural, judicial rightness), ahavat chesed (to love covenant faithfulness — chesed = loyal covenant love/kindness), and hatsnea lekhet im-Elohekha (to walk humbly with your God — tsana' = modest, reserved; lekhet = to walk consistently). These three encompass the totality of covenant ethics: justice in public life, covenant loyalty in relationships, and humble walk with God.
6.9: The voice of the LORD cries to the city — qol YHWH la'ir yiqra': the divine voice addressing the city directly; wisdom recognizes (tushiyyah yir'e shemekha — it is wisdom to fear your name). Shim'u matteh umi yeda'ah (hear, O tribe! who appointed the rod?) — the rod (matteh) as both the tribe and the instrument of punishment; YHWH appointed it, so hearing it is mandatory.
6.10: Are there yet treasures of wickedness in the house of the wicked — ha-'od ish bet rasha' otsarot resha': the rhetorical question expects a 'yes' answer — yes, they are still there. Ve-ephah razah zu'amah (and the short measure that is accursed) — ephah razah (lean/small ephah) — an undersized measuring container used to defraud in grain transactions; zu'amah (accursed, detested) — YHWH's emotional response to dishonest weights.
6.11: Shall I declare innocent the one who uses wicked scales — ha-ezkeh be-moznei resha': ezke (shall I acquit/declare innocent) — the legal verdict term. Mozne resha' (wicked/fraudulent scales) and kis avanei mirmah (a bag of deceitful weights) — the two instruments of commercial fraud: the scale beam and the standard weights (avanim, literally 'stones' used as weights). The legal-mercantile question: YHWH cannot issue a verdict of innocent when the instruments of trade are themselves dishonest.
6.12: Your wealthy people are full of violence — asher 'ashireha male'u chamas: chamas (violence, wrongdoing) filling the wealthy; the acquisition of wealth through force. Ve-yosheveha dibberu shaqer (your residents speak lies) — deceptive speech as standard commercial practice. U-leshsmonam remmiyah be-fihem (and their tongues are deceitful in their mouths) — remmiyah (deceit, treachery) describes the tongue as a standard tool of fraud.
6.13: Therefore I am striking you with a grievous blow — ve-gam ani hichleiti hakkotekha: YHWH has begun (hichleiti — begun, started) to strike; the judgment is underway. Hashimem 'al-chatta'otekha (devastating you because of your sins) — shalem (devastate, make desolate) as the consequence of chatta'ot (sins).
6.14: You shall eat, but not be satisfied, and there shall be hunger within you — atah tochal ve-lo tisba': the first covenant-curse reversal: eating without satisfaction (Lev 26:26; Deut 28:38-40). Ve-yeshchakha be-qirbeka (there shall be hunger within you) — the hunger comes from within despite consumption; a metaphor for insatiable greed that also becomes literal famine. Ve-tasseg ve-lo taflit (you shall put away goods, but not save them) — whatever you save, the sword takes.
6.15: You shall sow, but not reap; you shall press olives, but not anoint yourselves with oil; you shall tread grapes, but not drink the wine — the three agricultural covenant-curses of Deut 28:38-40 applied in sequence: grain, olive, vine. The pattern is labor without benefit: sow/not reap, press/not anoint, tread/not drink. These were the basis of Israelite prosperity; their inversion signals comprehensive covenant-curse fulfillment.
6.16: For the statutes of Omri are kept, and all the practices of the house of Ahab — ve-yishsamer chuqqot Omri: Omri (founder of the Omride dynasty, 876-869 BC) established Samaria and his dynasty's syncretistic religious policies; his son Ahab multiplied them. Kol-ma'aseh beit Achav (all the practices of the house of Ahab) — the Ahab era was the nadir of northern syncretism: Baal cult, Asherah worship, judicial murder (Naboth). Following their counsel (ba-attsotam telekhu) means Judah has adopted the same self-destructive trajectory. Le-sheim shemmah ve-livsheqetah 'ammi tissal (that I may make you a desolation, and your inhabitants a hissing) — shemmah (desolation, horror) and sheqetah (hissing — the sound made by onlookers at a ruined city, 1 Kgs 9:8).
